title: Results & Analysis
text:
This page presents the four main findings from our analysis of FILAH, TROUT, and the journalist dataset. Together, these results show how partial evidence can support conflicting accusations, and how broader context changes the interpretation of COOTEFOO’s behaviour.
Each section below highlights a different layer of the story, from accusation-based sentiment patterns to broader activity distribution, network reconstruction, and member-level sampling bias.

title: Results & Analysis
text:
FILAH & TROUT’s accusations against COOTEFOO
Our first analysis examines whether the accusations made by FILAH and TROUT are supported by the records contained within their own datasets. This addresses the first MC2 task: assessing whether signs of bias are visible in each group’s selective evidence.


FILAH’s Accusations

FILAH’s dataset suggests that COOTEFOO members show stronger overall positive sentiment toward tourism-related topics, while fishing-related topics appear weaker or more negatively viewed.


TROUT’s Accusations

TROUT’s dataset presents the opposite pattern, where members appear more supportive of fishing-related topics than tourism-related activities.


Taken separately, both datasets seem to support their respective accusations. However, this also reveals an important limitation: each narrative is shaped by the subset of records each group was able to capture. Rather than conclusively proving bias, these visuals show how selective evidence can produce two persuasive but opposing stories.

Key Takeaway
Both FILAH and TROUT’s datasets appear to support their own claims, but only within the boundaries of the evidence they selectively contain.

title: Results & Analysis
section: COOTEFOO Members’ Activity Distribution Across Topics
text:
COOTEFOO Members’ Activity Distribution Across Topics
Our second analysis looks at how COOTEFOO members distribute both their attention and sentiment across fishing- and tourism-related topics. This helps us move beyond accusation-based narratives and instead examine how the committee behaves more broadly when activity patterns are viewed in combination.



The first heatmap shows how COOTEFOO members allocate their time across different topics, while the second heatmap shows the sentiment they express toward those same topics.
Taken together, the two visuals suggest that tourism-related topics occupy a larger share of members’ attention and are also associated with more consistently positive sentiment. In contrast, fishing-related topics appear less evenly distributed across members and show a more mixed range of sentiment, suggesting weaker consensus and more variation in support.
This result adds an important layer to the earlier accusation analysis. Rather than relying only on the selective records presented by FILAH and TROUT, these broader topic-level patterns suggest that tourism is more dominant not only in where members spend their time, but also in how positively those topics are engaged with.


Key Takeaway
Tourism-related topics appear to dominate both member attention and sentiment, while fishing-related topics show lower engagement and more varied sentiment.

title: Results & Analysis
text:
Reconstructing the Full Picture
Our third analysis brings together the broader combined dataset to reconstruct a fuller picture of COOTEFOO’s behaviour. Unlike the earlier accusation-based visuals, this network graph captures how members, topics, and interactions connect when viewed in wider context.


The combined knowledge graph suggests that COOTEFOO is more tourism-leaning overall. Tourism-related topics appear more prominent in the network, with more members connected across multiple tourism activities and visibly stronger interaction patterns.
Three main patterns stand out:

Tourism dominates the network structure.
Tourism-related topics are more numerous and more widely connected across the graph. The edges linked to tourism topics also appear thicker and more frequent, suggesting stronger or more repeated interaction.
Tourism support is spread across more active members.
Members such as Tante Titan, Simone Kat, and Carol Limpet appear repeatedly across tourism-related nodes, indicating stronger and more consistent tourism engagement.
Fishing support is present, but more concentrated.
Fishing-related activity is still visible in the network, but it appears to be driven by a smaller subset of members, particularly Ed Helpsford and Teddy Goldstein.

Taken together, this full reconstruction provides a more balanced benchmark than the selective FILAH and TROUT datasets. Rather than showing two equally strong sides, the broader view suggests that tourism is more structurally dominant in COOTEFOO’s collective activity.


Key Takeaway
When the full dataset is reconstructed as a network, COOTEFOO appears more tourism-oriented overall, while fishing support is narrower and concentrated among fewer members.

title: Results & Analysis
section: Selective Accusations and Sampling Bias in TROUT and FILAH
text:
Selective Accusations and Sampling Bias in TROUT and FILAH
Our fourth analysis compares which members were accused by TROUT and FILAH against the broader benchmark of sentiment and record coverage. This helps us identify where each dataset selectively captures certain individuals while excluding others, and shows how sampling bias can distort both group-level conclusions and member-level accusations.


TROUT selectively captures a subset of members that makes fishing support appear stronger and tourism support appear weaker or more negative. In particular, it overrepresents members with stronger fishing sentiment while omitting members who show more consistently strong tourism support.



FILAH captures a different subset of members. While it includes some tourism-leaning individuals, it also omits important members whose broader records would complicate the accusation narrative. This makes its argument vulnerable to missing-context bias.

Seal is wrongly accused in both TROUT and FILAH, as his behavior shows no strong or consistent bias. His sentiment is low for both Tourism (0.133) and Fishing (0.083), well below the group averages, indicating a largely neutral stance. Unlike highly polarized individuals such as Teddy Goldstein and Ed Helpsford, Seal’s behavior is weak and based on limited records (12), making the accusation unsupported.
Tante Titan is the most impacted by sampling bias in the FILAH dataset. Despite having the highest Tourism sentiment (0.819) and the largest number of records (54), she is completely omitted. This removes the strongest and most consistent Tourism signal from the dataset, significantly weakening FILAH’s argument and distorting the overall interpretation.

Key takeaway
Both TROUT and FILAH rely on selective subsets of members. These omissions materially shape the conclusions they are able to draw, showing how incomplete coverage can create persuasive but misleading accusations.

title: Lessons Learnt
text: This page reflects on the key lessons, challenges, and practical skills we developed throughout the project. Beyond completing the analytical tasks, the process helped us better understand how visual analytics works in real settings, where incomplete data, design choices, and communication all shape the final story.

title: Lessons Learnt
text:
Key Lessons from the Project

Data can be persuasive without being complete
One of our biggest takeaways was that data does not automatically reveal the full truth. In this project, both FILAH and TROUT used partial datasets that seemed convincing when viewed on their own. However, once these were compared against the broader journalist dataset, it became clear that incomplete records could still produce strong but conflicting narratives. This taught us the importance of questioning what may be missing, not just what is visible.


Visual analytics is not only about charts
We also learnt that visual analytics is not simply about building graphs or dashboards. It is equally about structuring a clear analytical story. This includes deciding what to show first, how to compare different perspectives, and how to guide users from isolated findings toward a broader interpretation. Through this project, we learnt that good visual analytics combines exploration, explanation, and communication.


Broader context changes interpretation
A further lesson was that interpretation becomes much more reliable when more context is introduced. In our case, the fuller journalist dataset changed how we understood both the accusations and the individual members involved. This reminded us that conclusions drawn from narrow evidence can easily be overstated, and that broader context is often essential for balanced analysis.

title: Lessons Learnt
text:
Challenges We Faced

Working with raw and fragmented records
One major challenge was preparing the raw datasets for analysis. The project required us to work with multiple sources that differed in scope, format, and completeness. Before meaningful comparisons could be made, we had to organise the records, standardise labels, and build a structure that would allow us to analyse members, topics, and interactions consistently.


Turning complex findings into a clear story
Another challenge was deciding how to present our analysis without overwhelming the audience. Since the project involved multiple datasets, competing accusations, and different levels of analysis, there was a risk of producing too many visuals without a strong narrative. We had to think carefully about how to sequence the findings so that the audience could follow the logic from accusation, to reconstruction, to sampling bias.


Balancing depth with usability
We also found it challenging to balance analytical detail with readability. Some visuals contained rich information, but not all of it could be shown at once without making the page feel cluttered. This pushed us to make design decisions about layout, chart selection, and explanation length, so that the project would remain both informative and accessible.

title: Lessons Learnt
text:
Beyond the Lab

Learning to present analysis through a website
Beyond the lab sessions, we explored how to communicate our findings through a project website rather than a standard report alone. This involved working with Quarto, page layouts, section flow, image placement, navigation structure, and deployment through Vercel. Through this, we gained experience in presenting analytical work in a more interactive and user-friendly way.


Improving visual design and storytelling
We also went beyond the lab by thinking more carefully about how design influences understanding. This included refining chart layouts, improving readability, organising sections for smoother flow, and making the website visually consistent. These decisions helped us appreciate that design is not separate from analysis — it affects how findings are interpreted and remembered.


Strengthening collaboration through tools and workflow
Another area of learning beyond the lab was collaboration. As a group, we worked across different stages of the project, from data preparation to chart design to website building. This required us to coordinate files, align interpretations, and make sure that the final story remained consistent across pages. In doing so, we developed a better sense of how teamwork supports analytical projects.

title: Lessons Learnt
section: What We Would Improve with More Time
text:
What We Would Improve with More Time

Add deeper temporal analysis
If given more time, we would extend the project by exploring whether member behaviour changed over time. A temporal view of meetings, interactions, or topic engagement could reveal whether support for tourism or fishing strengthened, weakened, or shifted across different periods.


Incorporate more qualitative evidence
We would also explore ways to include more qualitative context, such as text-based reasons, discussion notes, or meeting records, where available. This could help us move beyond sentiment and interaction counts to better understand why certain members appeared more strongly connected to particular topics.


Expand interactivity and comparison features
A further improvement would be to make the website more interactive, for example by allowing users to switch views more easily between datasets or focus on specific members. This would make the project more exploratory and allow users to examine how the same story changes depending on what evidence is included.

title: Lessons Learnt
text:
Final Reflection
Overall, this project helped us understand that visual analytics is not only about finding patterns, but also about questioning evidence, recognising limitations, and presenting insights responsibly. It showed us that incomplete data can still produce confident-looking conclusions, and that careful comparison, reconstruction, and communication are essential in reaching a more balanced interpretation.

title: About Us
text:
We are a team of Singapore Management University students from the IS428 Visual Analytics course. This project brought together our shared interests in data storytelling, visual design, and analytical problem-solving.
Through this challenge, we worked collaboratively across data preparation, analysis, visualisation, and website development to uncover how selective evidence can shape competing narratives.

title: About Us
text:

title: About Us
text:
Our Project Experience
This project was a valuable opportunity for us to apply visual analytics concepts to a realistic and open-ended challenge. Beyond technical analysis, it taught us the importance of teamwork, critical thinking, and designing clear stories from incomplete data.

title: Project Overview
text:
An investigation into whether partial and selective data shaped accusations against COOTEFOO
Oceanus is undergoing a major economic transition. While fishing has long been central to the island’s identity and livelihood, tourism has expanded rapidly in recent years. As the island debates its future, COOTEFOO has come under pressure from two opposing groups making very different claims about its behaviour.
Our project examines whether those accusations hold up when partial advocacy datasets are compared against a broader journalist dataset. Through visual analytics, we reconstruct a fuller picture of COOTEFOO’s activities and explore how incomplete evidence can shape powerful, but conflicting, narratives.
Explore Methodology View Results & Analysis

title: Project Overview
text:
An investigation into whether partial and selective data shaped accusations against COOTEFOO
Oceanus is undergoing a major economic transition. While fishing has long been central to the island’s identity and livelihood, tourism has expanded rapidly in recent years. As the island debates its future, COOTEFOO has come under pressure from two opposing groups making very different claims about its behaviour.
Our project examines whether those accusations hold up when partial advocacy datasets are compared against a broader journalist dataset. Through visual analytics, we reconstruct a fuller picture of COOTEFOO’s activities and explore how incomplete evidence can shape powerful, but conflicting, narratives.
Explore Methodology View Results & Analysis

title: Project Overview
text:
The Problem
Both FILAH and TROUT rely on partial records of COOTEFOO members’ meetings, movement, and activities. Because neither side sees the full picture, each dataset highlights certain patterns while leaving others out. As a result, the same commission can appear tourism-leaning in one dataset and fishing-leaning in another.

Our task is to go beyond the accusations and reconstruct a more balanced view of COOTEFOO’s behaviour. Rather than taking either narrative at face value, we compare the selective datasets with the journalist’s broader dataset to understand what changes when more complete evidence is introduced.

title: Project Overview
text:
Datasets Used
Together, these four datasets allow us to compare competing narratives against a broader reconstruction of COOTEFOO’s activities.


FILAH
Partial dataset collected from the fishing advocacy perspective


TROUT
Partial dataset collected from the tourism advocacy perspective


Journalist
Broader dataset compiled by journalist Edwina Darling Moray


Road Map
Spatial dataset that provides geographic context across Oceanus

title: Project Overview
text:
Explore the Site
The project website is organised into three main sections. Each page focuses on a different part of the story, from how the data was prepared to what the final analysis reveals.


Methodology
See how the raw JSON records were cleaned, structured, and transformed into visuals for analysis.


Results & Analysis
Explore the main findings from our comparison of FILAH, TROUT, and the journalist dataset.


About Us
Meet the team behind the project and learn more about our roles and contributions.

title: Project Overview
text:
Our Analytical Focus
Across the site, our analysis centres on a few key questions:

- Do FILAH’s and TROUT’s accusations actually hold up based on the evidence they use?
- What changes when those claims are compared against the fuller journalist dataset?
- Does COOTEFOO, as a whole, appear biased toward tourism or fishing?
- Which members are most misrepresented when only partial data is used?

title: Project Overview
text:
Closing Note
At its core, this project is about the danger of drawing strong conclusions from incomplete evidence. Through visual analytics, we aim to show how different narratives can emerge from selective data, and how a more complete view can challenge, weaken, or sometimes support those narratives.
Explore the Methodology View Results & Analysis
